coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped waste biomass-derived carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for economical, green, rapid and sensitive detection of resveratrol in foods
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chenzhao
Yang, Jiawei
Gu, Xinru
Wang, Haiqiang
Wang, Xiujuan
Wang, Zhibing
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: Resveratrol (Res), a natural antioxidant widely found in fruits, plays a crucial role in preventing various diseases. However, traditional detection methods usually rely on large amounts of toxic solvents, leading to high costs and potential health risks to researchers. In this work, an economical, green, rapid, and sensitive method for Res detection was developed using banana peel-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BP-N-CDs) as fluorescent sensors. Banana peels, an abundant and renewable waste biomass, served as an excellent carbon source, providing economic benefits while promoting environmental sustainability. Using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the nitrogen source further enhanced the photoluminescent properties of BP-N-CDs, resulting in bright fluorescence and exceptional stability. Through the inner filter effect, Res rapidly quenched the fluorescence of BP-N-CDs within 10 s. This method demonstrated excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Res with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.21 ng/mL, and was successfully applied to food sample analysis. The greenness assessments further confirmed the eco-friendly and sustainable nature of the method. This work offered a novel and green approach for Res detection and provided valuable insights for the synthesis and optimization of biomass-derived carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensors
Biomass-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Green
Resveratrol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM and HR-TEM images, (B) particle size distribution, (C) XRD pattern, and (D) FT-IR spectra of the BP-N-CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of BP-N-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s, and (D) O 1 s.

(A) Lifetimes of the BP-N-CDs in the absence and presence of Res. (B) Three stern-volmer curves at different temperatures (30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C). (C) The excitation and emission spectra of the BP-N-CDs and the UV–vis absorption of Res. (D) Schematic diagram of quenching mechanism.
(A) Effects of Res concentrations (9.7 × 10−6 - 0.1 mg/mL) on the fluorescence intensity. (B) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Res. (C) Greenness assessment of the method.
BP-N-CDs as green fluorescent sensors for economical, green, rapid and sensitive detection of Res in foods.
Determination of Res in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped waste biomass-derived carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for economical, green, rapid and sensitive detection of resveratrol in foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resveratrol (Res), a natural antioxidant widely found in fruits, plays a crucial role in preventing various diseases. However, traditional detection methods usually rely on large amounts of toxic solvents, leading to high costs and potential health risks to researchers. In this work, an economical, green, rapid, and sensitive method for Res detection was developed using banana peel-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BP-N-CDs) as fluorescent sensors. Banana peels, an abundant and renewable waste biomass, served as an excellent carbon source, providing economic benefits while promoting environmental sustainability. Using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the nitrogen source further enhanced the photoluminescent properties of BP-N-CDs, resulting in bright fluorescence and exceptional stability. Through the inner filter effect, Res rapidly quenched the fluorescence of BP-N-CDs within 10 s. This method demonstrated excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Res with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.21 ng/mL, and was successfully applied to food sample analysis. The greenness assessments further confirmed the eco-friendly and sustainable nature of the method. This work offered a novel and green approach for Res detection and provided valuable insights for the synthesis and optimization of biomass-derived carbon dots.

Preparation and application of the Y-CDs.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Resveratrol (Res) is a natural polyphenolic compound abundant in fruits and has garnered significant attention due to its various pharmacological activities (Arora & Jaglan, 2017; Bhuia et al., 2024). As a prominent antioxidants in the human diet, Res exhibits strong free radical-scavenging abilities due to its hydroxyl groups and conjugated double bond system, providing important protective effects against numerous diseases, including cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, and neurodegenerative disorders (Bhuia et al., 2024; Li et al., 2023; Xia et al., 2016). However, excessive consumption of Res-rich fruits may lead to adverse effects, such as nausea, diarrhea, and liver dysfunction. This is due to the ability of Res to inhibit cytochrome P450 isoenzymes, which can affect the metabolism of various medications and increase health risks (Yu et al., 2003). Consequently, regulatory organizations have established requirements for the maximum allowable limits of Res in food. For instance, the maximum allowable limit for Res as a nutrient additive in liquor is 300 mg/L (China National Standard for Food Safety). Meanwhile, the European Commission Regulation (EU) 2017/2470 restricts Res in food supplements to 150 mg/day. From a food safety perspective, it is crucial to develop an accurate and sensitive method for detecting Res in foods.

Several methods have been used to detect Res, including electrochemical methods (Yang et al., 2021), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Gu et al., 2000), gas chromatography (GC) (Fabjanowicz et al., 2018), chemiluminescence (CL) (Wang & Zhang, 2007), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Moretón-Lamas et al., 2017), and LC-MS/MS (Ramalingam & Ko, 2016). Although these methods offer high sensitivity, high selectivity, and accurate quantification, they are often complex, require expensive equipment, and involve significant amounts of toxic organic solvents. This makes it challenging to align with the principles of green detection in food analysis. In contrast, the fluorescence-based method possesses broader application prospects due to its simplicity, high sensitivity, short detection time, and stability. Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of fluorescent materials (<10 nm), have gradually attracted the attention due to their easy preparation, excellent optical properties, and good biocompatibility (Wang, Wang, et al., 2023). CDs are gradually replacing traditional organic fluorescent dyes in food analysis due to their low toxicity (Venugopalan & Vidya, 2023). For example, Yuan et al. prepared dual-emission CDs using aminobenzenesulfonic acid and resorcinol through a hydrothermal method to analyze amaranthine red in food products (Yuan et al., 2023). However, most CDs are still prepared from chemical precursors, which makes it difficult to fully meet the requirements of green and non-toxicity (Chahal et al., 2021; Qandeel et al., 2023).

Waste biomass is widely present in nature, with readily available raw materials, high carbon content, and abundant surface functional groups, making it a promising precursor of CDs (Guo et al., 2024). Fluorescent CDs can be prepared from small molecules in waste biomass through high-temperature heating and some steps such as condensation, polymerization, and carbonization (Chahal et al., 2021). Among various types of waste biomass, waste banana peels contain proteins, lignin, and free sugars, making them an ideal carbon source for preparing CDs (Hikal et al., 2022). Wu et al. prepared CDs from banana peels to develop a fluorescent sensor, achieving tetracycline fluorescence sensing through the inner filter effect (Wu et al., 2023). Although this study demonstrated the feasibility of preparing CDs from banana peels, the luminescent performance of these CDs is still deficient, which restricts their application in analytical detection.

To enhance the luminescence performance of CDs, their fluorescence intensity and quantum yield can be improved by heteroatom doping (Zhou et al., 2016). Nitrogen (N) atoms are regarded as highly effective dopants due to their comparable size to carbon and their five valence electrons, which enable strong bonding with carbon atoms (An et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2019). Based on the above analysis, BP-N-CDs were prepared in this study using banana peels as the precursor and o-phenylenediamine as the dopant. The rapid detection of Res in real samples was achieved by the internal filtration effect (Scheme 1 ). Using banana peels as the precursor not only avoided the use of highly demanding chemicals but also facilitated the sustainable reuse of banana peel waste. More importantly, the doping of N atoms significantly enhanced the fluorescence performance of BP-N-CDs. Compared to other methods, the present approach was simple and rapid (<10 s), environmentally friendly, and highly sensitive (LOD = 2.21 ng/mL). This research not only provides reliable technical support for the green detection of Res but also offers a new idea for the development and application of nitrogen-doped biomass-derived carbon dots.

Resveratrol (Res), Anthocyanins (Ant), kaempferol (Kae), gallic acid (GA), rutin (Rut), chlorogenic acid (CA), pterostilbene (Pte), Cysteine(Cys), glutamic(Glu), histidine (His), vitamin A (VA), vitamin C (VC), glucose (Glu), and maltose (Mal) were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol, Al(OH)3·3H2O, KCl, CaCl2, MnCl2·4H2O, MgCl2, NaCl, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, FeCl3, FeSO4, K2CO3, Cr2(SO4)3, NiSO4, NaHCO3, AlPO4, diuron (Diu), monuron (Mon), soy isoflavones (SI), and piceatannol (Pic) were sourced from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Deionized water was produced using a Milli-Q water purification system. o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) was acquired from Beijing Chemical Factory. The bananas, grapes, blueberries, and peanuts needed for the experiments were purchased from a local supermarket.

HPLC analyses were performed using an Agilent Technologies 1100 series liquid chromatograph equipped with a quaternary pump and a diode array detector (DAD). The column was Thermo Fisher Scientific Acclaim120-C18 (5 μm, 4.6 mm × 250 mm). Transmission Electron Microscopy (JEOL, JEM-2100F) was utilized to obtain the images of BP-N-CDs. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted on a Thermo Fisher Nicolet IS50 spectrophotometer to analyze the functional groups. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Rigaku SmartLab-9 kW instrument over a 2θ range of 10° to 80° to assess the crystalline structure. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the elemental composition of BP-N-CDs. Additionally, the optical characteristics of the BP-N-CDs were evaluated by the SHIMADZU UVmini-280 spectrophotometer and the HORIBA FLUOROMAX-PLUS fluorometer.

The waste banana peels were dried and crushed. Subsequently, 0.30 g of banana peel powder and 0.03 g of OPD were placed into a reaction vessel. Then, 20 mL of ethanol was added. After the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 12 h, the liquid was cooled to room temperature. The large particles were removed by filtering through 1.5 μm filter paper, and the solution was centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was placed into a dialysis bag (500 Da) and dialyzed for 8 h. Then, the solution was filtered again using a 0.22 μm filter and freeze-dried to obtain a deep brown BP-N-CDs powder, which was subsequently used for characterization experiments.

Firstly, 200 μL of BP-N-CDs was added to 2.8 mL of Res standard solution at different concentrations and shaken for 10 s. The fluorescence spectrum was measured at the optimal excitation wavelength. Based on the relationship between Res concentration and fluorescence intensity, a standard curve was plotted and the sensitivity of the method was calculated. Subsequently, the same method was used to determine the fluorescence intensity of various interfering compounds (Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe2+, Mg2+, F−, Cys, Glu, His, Mal, Arg, VA, VC, Mon, and Diu) to assess the selectivity of BP-N-CDs.

Five food samples with different Res contents (two grapes and peanuts with different qualities and one blueberry) were selected as representative samples for analytical performance evaluation. The skins of grapes, blueberries, and peanuts were removed, and the pulp was crushed. Then, 100 mL of 85 % anhydrous ethanol was added to 5 g of samples. The mixtures were extracted in a water bath at 80 °C for 45 min. After extraction, the solutions were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter, and the volume was adjusted to 100 mL for subsequent experiments.

The analytes were separated using isocratic elution under the following chromatographic conditions: the mobile phases consisted of 45 % water (A) and 55 % acetonitrile (B), a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min, a monitoring wavelength of 306 nm, an injection volume of 10 μL, and a column temperature maintained at 30 °C for 20 min. Different concentrations of Res standards were detected by HPLC following these conditions, and standard curves were constructed. The five real samples described in subsection 2.5 were analyzed by HPLC under the same conditions, and the concentrations of Res in these samples were determined using the standard curves.

Briefly, 2.8 mL of the five real sample solutions were mixed with 200 μL of BP-N-CDs and shaken for 10 s. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer under optimal excitation wavelength conditions. The concentration of Res in the five food samples was then calculated using the standard curve.

The green level of the developed Res detection method was evaluated using the AGREEprep program. The assessment involved ten criteria, including the location of sample preparation, use of hazardous materials, sustainability and renewability of materials, waste generation, economic scale of the samples, sample throughput, use of automation, energy consumption, type of detection equipment, and operator safety. Finally, the average score was calculated based on these criteria to reflect the green level of the method.

The BP-N-CDs were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method using waste banana peels as the carbon source and OPD as the nitrogen source. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased after nitrogen doping compared to banana peels as the precursor alone. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of BP-N-CDs was 13.88 %, significantly higher than that of the undoped BP-CDs (8.75 %). The size and surface morphology of BP-N-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). TEM images showed that BP-N-CDs were uniformly dispersed with a spherical particle shape. The average particle diameter, based on measurements of 50 nanoparticles, was 3.14 ± 0.57 nm (Fig. 1A and Fig. 1B). HR-TEM images demonstrated distinct lattice fringes (0.17 nm). Additionally, the XRD pattern of BP-N-CDs (Fig. 1C) showed a broad peak around 2θ = 23.34°, corresponding to the graphite (002) plane. This indicated that BP-N-CDs possessed a graphitized structure, which is a typical characteristic of carbon dots (Wang, Li, et al., 2023).

FT-IR spectroscopy exhibited a peak at 3404 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH (Fig. 1D). Sharp peaks at 2962 cm−1 and 2857 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH in CH3 and CH2, indicating the presence of aliphatic hydrocarbons in bananas (Shi et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2024). The stretching vibration of CO at 1731 cm−1 and the bending vibration of CC at 1604 cm−1 appeared as two strong absorption peaks (Amjadi et al., 2016; Bei et al., 2023). Additionally, CH bending vibrations and CO stretching vibrations were recognized at 1405 cm−1 and 1037 cm−1 (Guo et al., 2024; Padmapriya et al., 2023), respectively. XPS spectroscopy was utilized to investigate the surface elemental composition and binding energy of BP-N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, three distinct characteristic peaks appeared at 284.8 eV, 401.6 eV, and 532.1 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, with elemental ratios of C, N, and O being 67.54 %, 3.73 %, and 28.72 %, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum of BP-N-CDs was deconvoluted into three components at 284.6 eV (CC), 286 eV (CN), and 288.2 eV (C=O) (Fig. 2B) (Liao et al., 2015). In addition, the N 1 s spectrum was fitted with two peaks, confirming the presence of two nitrogen states: Pyrrole-N (399.4 eV) and Pyrolic-N (399.8 eV) (Fig. 2C) (Ma et al., 2020). The O 1 s spectrum was split into two peaks at 531.6 and 532.8 for CO and CO (Fig. 2D) (Lv et al., 2020). These XPS results corroborated the findings of the FT-IR analysis.

As shown in Fig. S2A, UV–Vis results revealed an absorption peak at 275 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of BP-N-CDs. A strong absorption peak at 306 nm was attributed to the n-π electronic transitions of the CO groups (Uriarte et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2023). When BP-N-CDs were excited at 360 nm, the highest emission peak appeared at 440 nm. The inset illustrated the luminescent states of the BP-N-CDs solution under visible light (pale yellow) and 365 nm UV light (bule). To further explore the optical characteristics of BP-N-CDs, emission wavelengths were measured under various excitation wavelengths. As demonstrated in Fig. S2B, the emission wavelength progressively shifted to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased, indicating the excitation-dependent characteristics of BP-N-CDs. This behavior was attributed to changes in the size, surface morphology, and functional groups of BP-N-CDs due to the incorporation of heteroatoms (Cai et al., 2018). It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of BP-N-CDs did not significantly decrease at the maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm after the addition of Res. However, a marked decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed when excited at 350 nm. This is because the excitation spectrum at 350 nm exhibited greater overlap with the absorption spectrum of Res compared to that at 360 nm (Fig. S3). By comparing the fluorescence quenching rates (Q = (F0 F)/F0 × 100 %) at different excitation wavelengths after adding Res, the highest quenching rate was found at 350 nm (Fig. S4). Since a higher quenching rate enhances detection sensitivity, 350 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments in this study.

To address the complexity of the detection environment for real samples, an effective detection system requires good stability. The fluorescence stability of BP-N-CDs was thoroughly evaluated to determine their suitability for practical applications. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of BP-N-CDs remained above 80 % within the pH range of 1.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S5A). Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity of BP-N-CDs significantly decreased in strong alkaline environments due to the deprotonation process (Ehtesabi et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S5B, the fluorescence intensity of BP-N-CDs remained at a high level even at a NaCl concentration of 1 mol/L, demonstrating their capability to detect target substances in high-ionic-strength environments. The BP-N-CDs maintained a high fluorescence intensity after being heated at 60 °C for 10 min, indicating their applicability in high-temperature environments (Fig. S5C). Additionally, BP-N-CDs demonstrated little change in fluorescence intensity after 10 min of irradiation under UV light (Fig. S5D). In brief, the BP-N-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence performance even under various extreme conditions, which ensured their potential and applicability in food analysis.

To research the interaction mechanism between BP-N-CDs and Res, the fluorescence lifetime of BP-N-CDs was examined in the presence and absence of Res. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence lifetimes of BP-N-CDs remained consistent in both cases (τ0 = 3.23 ns, τ = 3.19 ns, τ0/τ = 1.01), which ruled out the possibility of dynamic quenching and FRET (Fig. 3A). Further exploration was carried out by comparing the UV absorption spectra of BP-N-CDs before and after the addition of Res. The UV absorption spectra of BP-N-CDs after the addition of Res exhibited a significant red shift in the absorption peak at 275 nm, suggesting that this could be caused by static quenching (Fig. S6). XPS analysis revealed the presence of pyrrolic N (399.4 eV) and pyrazinic N (399.8 eV) in BP-N-CDs (Fig. 2C), which could form hydrogen bonds with the phenolic hydroxyl groups (OH) of Res. Additionally, π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic ring of Res and the aromatic structure of BP-N-CDs explained the red shift in the absorption peak (275 nm). The surface chemistry of BP-N-CDs provided multiple binding sites for resveratrol, supporting the static quenching mechanism at the molecular level. The fluorescence quenching constants from the Stern-Volmer curves decreased gradually with increasing temperatures (30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C). The Ksv values at these temperatures were 0.0781, 0.0675, and 0.0572 μg/mL, respectively, indicating that the quenching mechanism was static (Fig. 3B). Moreover, the UV absorption of Res partially overlapped with the excitation spectrum of BP-N-CDs (Fig. 3C), which suggested that the quenching mechanism might be the inner filter effect (IFE). This was further confirmed by calculating the Parker eqs. S1, S2, and S3. The values of Fcor/Fobsd, Eobsd, and Ecor gradually increased with increasing Res concentration, which further confirmed the presence of IFE (Table S1). In summary, the quenching of BP-N-CDs fluorescence was caused by the synergistic effect of static quenching and the IFE (Fig. 3D).

The selectivity of BP-N-CDs towards different ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe2+, Mg2+, F−, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, NO3−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, and PO4 3−, biomolecules (Cys, His, and Arg), common compounds (Mal, Glu, VC, VE, Ant, Kae, GA, Rut, CA, Pte, SI, and Pic), and potential pesticide residues (Diu and Mon) was studied. As shown in Fig. S7, these substances had negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity. However, the presence of Res caused a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity, indicating that BP-N-CDs can selectively detect Res even in the presence of interfering substances. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of BP-N-CDs decreased with increasing concentration of Res (9.7 × 10−6-0.1 mg/mL). A linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Res (Fig. 4A), with the linear equation being Y = −6,753,394× + 1,194,428 and R2 = 0.9997 (Fig. 4B). The LOD was calculated to be 2.21 ng/mL based on the formula 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity of 11 blank samples and k is the slope of the standard curve). The reaction between BP-N-CDs and Res was completed rapidly within just 10 s, further indicating the potential of BP-N-CDs as a quick fluorescent sensor for Res detection (Fig. S8). In summary, this method demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity for Res detection, along with a short detection time.

Two varieties of grapes, peanuts, and one blueberry sample were selected as real samples to assess the applicability of the proposed method. The spiked experiments were conducted at five different concentrations (0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 μg/mL) to evaluate the accuracy of the present method. Each concentration was prepared with three replicates. As demonstrated in Table 1 , the spiked recoveries of Res ranged from 96.55 % to 108.00 %, with RSD as low as 0.87 % to 2.97 %. Using a genuine Res standard as a reference, the Res in the five food samples exhibited corresponding peaks around 7.6 min, confirming the presence of endogenous Res in these samples (Fig. S9). A statistical check for co-linearity between the HPLC method and the proposed method was performed, and the results indicated a strong correlation between the two, with an R2 value of 0.9999 (Fig. S10). Notably, the detection time of the proposed method was significantly shorter than that of the HPLC method. These results indicated that BP-N-CDs exhibited good analytical performance, providing a reliable and efficient approach for detecting Res in real food samples.

The proposed method was compared with others for detecting Res in terms of sample complexity, linear range, and LOD. The results demonstrated that this method was not only suitable for more complex samples but also exhibited a low LOD and a wide linear range (Table S2). To validate the green and environmentally friendly characteristics of this method, a greenness assessment was conducted. The results were visualized through a circular chart, reflecting the overall eco-friendliness of the method. The rating scores ranged from 0 to 1, where 0 denoted poor eco-friendliness performance on all standards, and 1 represented complete compliance with green chemistry principles. As shown in Fig. 4C, this method primarily used sustainable and renewable materials (banana peels) and avoided hazardous materials. The operational steps were simple, with a small sample volume and material waste. Furthermore, the method demonstrated low energy consumption and posed no hazards to personnel. The average green score calculated by the AGREEprep was 0.85, aligning with the greenness criteria reported in other studies (Kavalchuk et al., 2024; Salem AlSalem et al., 2024). These findings highlight the method's strong eco-friendliness and sustainability.

In conclusion, BP-N-CDs were prepared for the first time for the rapid detection of Res in complex food matrices. The fluorescence intensity of BP-N-CDs was quenched by Res within 10 s through the IFE. The BP-N-CDs exhibited excellent performance, including a green preparation process, short detection time, high sensitivity, and good selectivity. Notably, the prepared fluorescent sensor was successfully used for the Res detection in food samples (grapes, peanuts, and blueberries) and satisfactory results were obtained. Furthermore, this method utilized readily available and renewable waste biomass, ensuring high safety and adherence to green chemistry principles. This work not only offered a new approach for Res detection but also provided valuable theoretical support for the further application of green carbon dots in food analysis.

Chenzhao Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jiawei Yang: Validation, Methodology. Xinru Gu: Validation, Methodology. Haiqiang Wang: Validation, Methodology. Xiujuan Wang: Validation, Methodology. Zhibing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology. Guoying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20220101350JC).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.142886.
